coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional fluorescent molecularly imprinted resin based on carbon dot for selective detection and enrichment of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid in lettuce
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yunjia
Huang, Chao
Ma, Shujuan
Bo, Chunmiao
Gong, Bolin
Ou, Junjie
coredata.dc:description: The work provided a method for synthesizing a simple fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer by surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) and its application in real sample. Poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) microspheres were selected as a matrix, 4-vinylpyridine, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) as functional monomer, cross-linker and template molecule, respectively, to fabricate MAR@MIP with core–shell structure. For comparison, carbon dot (CD) as a fluorescence source was synthesized with o-phenylenediamine and tryptophan as precursors via hydrothermal method and integrated into MIP to acquire MAR@CD-MIP. MAR@CD-NIP was also prepared without adding the template molecule. The adsorption capacity of MAR@CD-MIP reached 104 mg g−1 for 2,4-D, which was higher than that of MAR@MIP (60 mg g−1). However, the adsorption capacity of MAR@CD-NIP was only 13.2 mg g−1. The linear range of fluorescence detection for 2,4-D was 18–72 μmol/L, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.35 μmol/L. The fluorescent MAR@CD-MIP was successfully applied in enrichment of lettuce samples. The recoveries of the three spiked concentrations of 2,4-D in lettuce were tested by fluorescence spectrophotometry and ranged in 97.3–101.7 %. Meanwhile, the results were also verified by HPLC. As a result, bi-functional molecularly imprinted resin was successfully fabricated to detect and enrich 2,4-D in real samples, and exhibited good selectivity, sensitivity and great application prospect in food detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid
Molecular imprinting
Fluorescence detection
Carbon dots
Surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Isothermal and kinetic adsorption of materials
Fluorescence selectivity and sensitivity detection of 2,4-D using MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@CD-NIP
Possible mechanism of CD fluorescence quenching
Application of MAR@MIP-CD in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Research and Development Program of Ningxia

Gong，as well as the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974137), the CAS-Weigao Research & Development Program ([2017]-009) and the National Natural Science Foundation of Ningxia (No. 2021AAC02017) to J. Ou.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Adsorption (a) isothermal and (b) kinetic curves of MAR@CD-MIP, MAR@CD-NIP and MAR@MIP.
High-resolution XPS spectra (a, d) C 1 s, (b, d) N 1 s and (c, f) O 1 s of (a-c) MAR@CD-MIP and (d-f) MAR@MIP.
Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) MAR@CD-MIP and (c) MAR@CD-NIP with the addition of 2,4-D and corresponding fluorescence intensity Stern-Volmer plots of (b) MAR@CD-MIP and (d) MAR@CD-NIP.
Chromatograms of (a) standard sample of 2, 4-D, (b) lettuce sample, (c) spiked-lettuce sample and (d) after enrichment by MAR@CD-MIP.
Spiked recoveries for determination of 2,4-D in lettuce with fluorescence spectrophotometry and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional fluorescent molecularly imprinted resin based on carbon dot for selective detection and enrichment of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid in lettuce

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory for Chemical Engineering and Technology, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, North Minzu University, Yinchuan 750021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory for Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory for Chemical Engineering and Technology, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, North Minzu University, Yinchuan 750021, China (Junjie Ou).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory for Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The work provided a method for synthesizing a simple fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer by surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) and its application in real sample. Poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) microspheres were selected as a matrix, 4-vinylpyridine, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) as functional monomer, cross-linker and template molecule, respectively, to fabricate MAR@MIP with core–shell structure. For comparison, carbon dot (CD) as a fluorescence source was synthesized with o-phenylenediamine and tryptophan as precursors via hydrothermal method and integrated into MIP to acquire MAR@CD-MIP. MAR@CD-NIP was also prepared without adding the template molecule. The adsorption capacity of MAR@CD-MIP reached 104 mg g−1 for 2,4-D, which was higher than that of MAR@MIP (60 mg g−1). However, the adsorption capacity of MAR@CD-NIP was only 13.2 mg g−1. The linear range of fluorescence detection for 2,4-D was 18–72 μmol/L, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.35 μmol/L. The fluorescent MAR@CD-MIP was successfully applied in enrichment of lettuce samples. The recoveries of the three spiked concentrations of 2,4-D in lettuce were tested by fluorescence spectrophotometry and ranged in 97.3–101.7 %. Meanwhile, the results were also verified by HPLC. As a result, bi-functional molecularly imprinted resin was successfully fabricated to detect and enrich 2,4-D in real samples, and exhibited good selectivity, sensitivity and great application prospect in food detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: The problem of pesticide residues in the food is becoming more and more serious with the development of agriculture (Fenner, et al., 2013; Li, et al., 2021; Lu, et al., 2015). As a plant growth regulator, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) has been widely used as an herbicide in food crops since it was commercialized in 1944 (Rani, et al., 2021). It has been reported that long-term exposure to 2,4-D can cause DNA damage, affect the endocrine system (Jaafarzadeh, Ghanbari, & Ahmadi, 2017; Md Meftaul, et al., 2020) and is carcinogenic to some extent (Islam, et al., 2018). The WHO sets a maximum level of 2,4-D contamination in drinking water of 30 μg L−1, and the maximum residual amount of 2,4-D in cabbage is less than 0.05 mg kg−1 by EU (Sheng, et al., 2017). Up to now, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography, electrochemical sensor and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry have been applied in the quantitative detection of 2, 4-D in food (Arduini, et al., 2019; Huang, et al., 2021; Morton, et al., 2019; Vdovenko, et al., 2013). However, these analytical methods always require complex pretreatment process and are time-consuming, and even require expensive equipment (Ansari & Masoum, 2021). It is necessary to find simple and selective method for detection and enrichment of trace 2,4-D residue in actual samples (Li, et al., 2020; Morton, et al., 2019).

The fluorescence detection method has no complicated sample pre-treatment and is highly sensitive, making it an ideal alternative to HPLC in the detection of 2,4-D (Yan, Li, & Su, 2018). In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) and quantum dots have attracted extensive attention due to their excellent performance in fluorescence detection and biological imaging (Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015). Recently, Jia et al. successfully fabricated novel fluorescent molecular imprinting sensors that specifically recognize 2, 4-D by immobilizing CdTe quantum dots on the surface of mesoporous microspheres by combining quantum dot technology with surface imprinting technology, and applied them in real samples (Jia, et al., 2017). The CDs is a new kind of carbon-based nanomaterial (Xu, et al., 2004). Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots, there is no need to use toxic heavy metals in the preparation process of CDs (Molaei, 2019), which makes the carbon sources for the preparation of carbon dots wider. Macroporous adsorption resin (MAR) is a kind of spherical resin with a network structure and a large specific surface area (Bolisetty, Peydayesh, & Mezzenga, 2019; Tang, et al., 2017). Due to its good structural stability under complex conditions, good adsorption capacity and easy surface modification, it was widely applied in the field of food (Pi, et al., 2008). By using MAR as a substrate and modifying its surface, our group has synthesized various of functionalized materials (Cai, et al., 2021; Guo, et al., 2022; Zhou, et al., 2020). More recently, our group achieved bifunctional adsorption and detection of metal ions by grafting carbon dots with sulfhydryl groups onto the MAR surface through “thiol-yne” reaction (Huang, et al., 2022). Other than that, Zhu et al. successfully synthesized a photosensitive imprinted polymer based on red emitting CD by surface imprinting method, and successfully applied it in the detection of lambda-cyhalothrin (Zhu, et al., 2021). Numerous studies had been conducted to show that the optical sensor prepared by combining CD and MIP has the advantages of both high sensitivity of CD and high selectivity of MIP (Diaz-Alvarez & Martin-Esteban, 2021).

Inspired by these studies, a fluorescent molecularly imprinted resin (MAR@CD-MIP) was synthesized by combining surface imprinting technique with CD. The prepared CD with amino group was doped into the imprinting layer, and fluorescent quenching with 2,4-D occurred to realize the detection of 2,4-D. Meanwhile, 2,4-D could be selectively recognized by MAR@CD-MIP. The binding ability, adsorption time and fluorescence properties of MAR@CD-MIP were investigated to simultaneously achieve detection and enrichment of 2,4-D in lettuce.

2,4-D, 4-vinylpyridine (4-VP), ethylene dimethacrylate (EDMA), glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), 4-dimethylaminopyridine, 2-bromoisobutyryl bromide, 2,2′-bipyridyl (Bpy), azodiisobutyronitrile, tryptophan, o-phenylenediamine, 1,4-dicarboxybenzene, 2-aminoterephthalic acid, 5-chlorosalicylic acid, 1,2-diaminobenzene and CuBr were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), ethanol, polyvinyl alcohol, sodium dodecyl sulfate, tetrahydrofuran (THF), triethylamine (TEA) and methanol were purchased from Damao (Tianjin, China). The above reagents were not further purified. The lettuce was bought from the local supermarket (Yinchuan, China). The water used in the experiment was of ultrapure.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were taken by JEM-7500F (JEOL-Japan). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken by HT7700 microscopy (JEOL-Japan). Elemental analysis was measured by Vario EL Ⅲ system (Illermann, Germany). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) was measured by instrument of Shimadzu Corporation (Japan). The weight loss rate of material was measured with PerkinElmer Pyris 1 TGA analyzer (PE, USA) at a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1. The specific surface area of the material was analyzed with ASAP2020HD88 automatic specific surface area and micropore pore analysis (Max, USA). The adsorption data were measured by Shunyu UV-2800 s spectrometer (Beijing, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed by ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The fluorescence spectrum of the material was analyzed by LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

Yellowish CDs were prepared according to previous report (Ru, et al., 2021). Tryptophan (0.105 g) and o-phenylenediamine (0.075 g) were dissolved into 50 mL water and then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a capacity of 100 mL. After heated at 160 ℃ for 7 h, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to obtain a yellow solution of CDs.

First, according to our previous report (Hao, et al., 2011), poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) (noted poly(GMA-co-EDMA)) microspheres were prepared by one-step seeded swelling polymerization. Next, the poly(GMA-co-EDMA) (3 g) microspheres were dispersed in sulfuric acid aqueous solution and reacted for 8 h. The obtained product was washed with water until neutral, and dried to obtain acidified poly(GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres.

Second, the acidified poly(GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were dispersed in 15 mL of dichloromethane and stirred for 10 min. Next, triethylamine (0.5 mL), 2-bromoisobutyl bromide (0.5 mL) and 4-dimethylaminopyridine (5 mg) were added and stirred for 10 min, and the reaction was carried out in an ice bath for 2 h. Then, the reaction was carried out at room temperature for 24 h. The obtained product was washed 3 times with dichloromethane, water and ethanol, successively, and dried to obtain the macromolecular initiator MAR-Br.

Finally，the specifically recognized MAR@CD-MIP was synthesized via SI-ATRP. In brief, MAR-Br (0.5 g), 4-VP (85 μL), 2,4-D (55 mg), EDMA (282 μL), concentrated CD solution (50 μL) and Bpy (25 mg) were dispersed in 20 mL methanol in sequence. And then, the device was sealed and freezing, evacuating and thawing operations were performed 3 times. CuBr was quickly added under the condition of nitrogen gas, and the freezing, evacuating and thawing operations were further performed 3 times, and then the reaction was carried out at 60 °C for 8 h under nitrogen protection. The product was washed three times with EDTA-2Na (0.1 mol/L) solution and then washed to neutral with ultrapure water, and vacuum dried for 12 h. The obtained product was extracted with Soxhlet to remove the template using methanol-glacial acetic acid (8/2, v/v) mixture as the eluent. The product was washed 3 times with methanol and dried under vacuum for 24 h to obtain MAR@CD-MIP.

For comparison, non-imprinted resin (assigned as MAR@CD-NIP) was prepared under the same conditions as described above, but without adding template molecule (2,4-D). Additionally, a non-fluorescent material (noted MAR@MIP) was similarly synthesized without adding CD.

Dissolved a certain amount of 2,4-D into ultrapure water to prepare mother solution (160 mg L−1) for future experiment. A series of standard solutions with the concentration of 8–160 mg L−1 were acquired. Then, the absorbance was measured at 282 nm with these standard solutions to obtain the standard curve. In order to study the adsorption properties of MAR@CD-MIP, MAR@CD-NIP and MAR@MIP, isothermal adsorption and kinetic adsorption experiments were performed.

In isothermal adsorption experiment, 5 mg of materials were added into 40 mL of 2,4-D standard solution with different concentrations (16, 32, 48, 64, 96, 128 and 160 mg L−1). The mixture was oscillated at 25 ℃, 200 r min−1 for 3 h. The sample was obtained by 0.22 μm filtration membrane. The concentration of 2,4-D was measured by UV spectrometry at 282 nm. The adsorption capacity (Q e) was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Q e
= V
C 0
-
C e
/ m
where V (L) is the volume of 2,4-D solution; C 0 and C e (mg L−1) are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of 2,4-D in the adsorbed solution, respectively, and m (g) is the mass of the adsorbed material.

Isothermal adsorption curve shows the relationship between different concentration and adsorption capacity at the same temperature. Langmuir adsorption isotherm model and Freundlich adsorption isothermal model were used to study the results of isothermal adsorption.

The Langmuir adsorption isotherm model is shown by the following:
(2)
C e
/
Q e
=
C e
/
Q
max
+ 1 /
K L
Q
max
where Q max (mg g−1) is theoretical maximum adsorption capacity and K L(L mg−1) is the Langmuir constant.

The Freundlich adsorption isothermal model is an empirical formula which considers that the surface of adsorbent has uneven surface energy. The Freundlich model is shown by the following:
(3)
ln
Q e
= l n
K f
+ 1 / n
ln
C e
where K f (mg g−1) is the Freundlich constant and n is the intensity of the adsorption.

In kinetic adsorption experiments, 5 mg of materials were added to 10 mL of 2,4-D standard solution (160 mg L−1). The mixture was oscillated at 25 ℃, 200 r min−1 for 5, 10, 20, 30, 60, 90 and 120 min. Then, the samples were treated and tested using the same method as the isotherm adsorption experiment. Kinetic adsorption represents the change of adsorption capacity over time. It is generally explained by pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order dynamics models. The pseudo-first-order dynamics models are shown by the following:
(4)
l n
Q e
-
Q t
= l n
Q e
-
k 1
t
where Q t (mg g−1) represents the adsorption capacity at different times; k 1 is the pseudo-first-order kinetic model constant and t (min) is the adsorption time.

The pseudo-second-order dynamics models are shown by the following:
(5)
t /
Q t
= 1 /
k 2
Q e 2
+ t /
Q e
where k 2 is the pseudo-second-order kinetic model constant.

Material suspensions (0.5 mg mL−1) were prepared by dispersing a certain amount of MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@CD-NIP in water. Different concentrations (0–16 mg L−1) of 2,4-D standard solutions were also prepared for fluorescence detection. The solution of 2,4-D (9 mL) with different concentrations was mixed with MAR@CD-MIP/NIP suspension (1 mL), which was shaken to make it fully mixed, and its fluorescence intensity was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometry.

Fresh lettuce was bought from a local supermarket (Yinchuan, China) as the real sample. The lettuce (40 g) was juiced with a household juicer, then acetonitrile (200 mL) was added and stirred for 10 min, then refined for 30 min. 2,4-D was added into the sample to prepare spiked samples with the concentrations of 0.64, 1.48 and 6.4 μg kg−1, respectively. Then, the mixture was filtered to obtain the real sample.

A certain amount of MAR@CD-MIP was added to the self-made SPE column, and then 10 mL of above sample were added. The flow rate was controlled to make the target substance fully contact with the material. The ultrapure water was selected to wash interfering substances that may affect the experimental results. Finally, 10 mL methanol-acetic acid (6/4, v/v) were used to eluate the target substance on the material. The eluent was collected, dried by nitrogen blowing instrument and redissolved by methanol (0.1 mL) for analysis of HPLC or fluorescent spectrophotometry. The HPLC system was composed of an SPD-20A UV–visible detector and two LC-20-AT pumps (Shimadzu, Japan). HPLC conditions: a Daiso C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm, 5 µm, Daiso Technologies Inc. Beijing, China); mobile phase, methanol-1 % trifluoroacetic acid aqueous solution (65/35, v/v); flow rate, 1.0 mL min−1; detection wavelength, 282 nm; injection volume, 20 μL; column temperature, 25 °C.

CD was an emerging nanoscale carbon-based material that possesses excellent fluorescent properties, but its anti-interference ability was poor (Zhu, Yuan, Han, Liu, & Sun, 2021). Moreover, molecular imprinting was a material with specific adsorption that allowed rapid and selective enrichment in the presence of template molecules (Ansari & Masoum, 2021). Immobilization of CD in molecular imprinting was expected to allow materials with both sensitivity of CD and selectivity of molecular imprinting. The preparation of MAR@CD-MIP is schematically shown in Fig. 1
. Poly(GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were acidified as a matrix and halogen atoms were introduced to the surface of microspheres by adding 2-bromoisobutyryl bromide. Then, 4-VP, CD, 2,4-D, EDMA and CuBr act as functional monomers, template molecule, crosslinker and catalyst for SI-ATRP reactions, respectively. The 2,4-D was bonded with 4-VP via hydrogen bonds or interaction and wrapped by EDMA around the substrate surface. Meanwhile, amino groups on the surface of CD could also generate hydrogen bonds with carboxyl group in 2,4-D, so doped CD was expected to improve the adsorption capacity of MAR@CD-MIP. Compared with general MIP materials, MAR@CD-MIP would introduce CD as a functional monomer into the imprinting layer, and CD could offer fluorescent detection and selective enrichment of 2,4-D in actual samples.

The TEM image of CD is shown in Fig. S1 . CD was approximately spherical with an average particle size of 2.7 nm. The TEM images of MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@CD-NIP are given in Fig. S2 , it could be observed from Fig. S2 b and e that the CD was wrapped in the imprinted layer of MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@CD-NIP. Additionally, the high resolution TEM images of MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@CD-NIP (Fig. S2 c and f) exhibited that the lattice fringe of CD was 0.21 nm, which is attributed to the in-plane lattice distance of graphene (100 facet). The morphologies of poly(GMA-co-EDMA), MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@CD-NIP were characterized by SEM. Mono-dispersed poly(GMA-co-EDMA) sphere with about 4 ∼ 5 μm in diameter were presented in Fig. S3 a and b. Compared with pristine poly(GMA-co-EDMA) resin, the as-prepared MAR@CD-MIP (Fig. S3 c and d) and MAR@CD-NIP (Fig. S3 e and f) became rougher. As shown in Table S1 , the specific surface area of poly(GMA-co-EDMA) resin was 27.3 m2 g−1, while that of MAR@CD-MIP was decreased to 18.2 m2 g−1. Additionally, the pore size of MAR@CD-MIP was mainly distributed between 10 and 80 nm (Fig. S3 g and h), and larger pore size would be beneficial to improve the mass transfer rate during adsorption.

In order to prove successful synthesis of MAR@CD-MIP, the material was characterized by elemental analysis, TG analysis and FT-IR. The elemental analysis of MAR@CD-MIP, MAR@MIP and poly(GMA-co-EDMA) was shown in Table S2 . The contents of C and H elements in poly(GMA-co-EDMA) were more abundant, while the content of N element was only 0.19 %. Compared with the pristine microspheres, the contents of C and H in MAR@CD-MIP increased to 51.16 % and 6.62 %, and the content of N element increased from 0.19 % to 0.45 %, respectively. The contents of C and N in MAR@MIP were more abundant than those in pristine microspheres. Additionally, the C and H contents of MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@MIP were not different, and the N content of MAR@CD-MIP slightly increased from 0.37 % to 0.45 % after integration of CD.

Fig. S4 a presents the FT-IR spectra of these materials. Two absorption peaks at 3400 and 3472 cm−1 emerged in CD, which was characteristic absorption peaks of amino group. The absorption peak at 910 cm−1 belonged to the characteristic peak of epoxy in poly(GMA-co-EDMA). The characteristic peaks at 3400 and 3472 cm−1 of amino group could be clearly observed in the MAR@CD-MIP containing CD, but not in the MAR@MIP without CD modification. Meanwhile, both MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@MIP possessed the characteristic peak of carbonyl bond (CO) at 1728 cm−1, which came from the pristine poly(GMA-co-EDMA) resin, and proved the successful synthesis of the materials.

As shown in Fig. S4 b, poly(GMA-co-EDMA) and MAR@CD-MIP were analyzed by measuring weight loss in the temperature range of 100–800 ℃. Both poly(GMA-co-EDMA) and MAR@CD-MIP exhibited thermal stability before 230 ℃. When the temperature was higher than 230 ℃, the mass of MAR@CD-MIP began to lose. In the range of 230–350 ℃, the organic matter grafted on the surface of MAR@CD-MIP began to decompose rapidly, and the decomposition rate slows down at 350–450 ℃. The surface material is completely decomposed at 800 ℃, and the weight loss rate reaches 95 %. For poly(GMA-co-EDMA), the weight loss was 88 % at 800 ℃. Obviously, the weight loss of MAR@CD-MIP was higher than that of poly(GMA-co-EDMA). This was due to the decomposition of the imprinting layer on the surface of MAR@CD-MIP. The above results further indicated that MAR@CD-MIP was successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isothermal and kinetic adsorption of materials
ce:section.ce:para:
Isothermal adsorption reflects the materials adsorption performance of 2,4-D. As shown in Fig. 2
a, the adsorption properties of materials were investigated in the concentration range of 16–160 mg L−1. In the concentration range of 16–64 mg L−1, the adsorption capacity of MAR@CD-MIP increased rapidly with an increase of 2,4-D concentration and then increased slowly, and even the adsorption equilibrium of MAR@CD-MIP was achieved at 160 mg L−1 and the adsorption capacity was 104 mg g−1. For MAR@CD-NIP, the change in adsorption capacity at concentrations of 0–64 mg L−1 was consistent with that MAR@CD-MIP. However, the adsorption capacity only was 12 mg g−1. The adsorption isotherm of MAR@MIP reached the plateau when the 2,4-D concentration was higher than 90 mg L−1, and the highest adsorption capacity of MAR@MIP was 60 mg g−1. Both Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms were most classical model to describe adsorption process. The result was shown in Table S3 . It could be observed that Langmuir adsorption isothermal model (R = 0.9978) was more appropriate for this situation than Freundlich adsorption isothermal model (R = 0.9713) in the system of MAR@CD-MIP adsorption. Langmuir adsorption isotherm model assumes that the surface properties of the adsorbent were homogeneous, and each active site adsorbs one target substance, and the adsorption was monolayer. As a result, the adsorption on MAR@CD-MIP was mainly monolayer adsorption.

Kinetic adsorption is one of the important means to investigate the adsorption properties of materials. As shown in Fig. 2 b, the kinetic adsorption of materials was performed in 0–120 min. It could be found that the adsorption capacity of MAR@CD-MIP was rapidly increased within 10 min, and the adsorption capacity reached saturation in 10–60 min. In the case of MAR@CD-NIP, the adsorption capacity was rapidly increased within 10 min and reached the plateau at 30 min. For MAR@MIP, the adsorption capacity was rapidly increased within 20 min and reached the plateau at 30 min. The results of kinetic adsorption were analyzed by pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models and shown in Table S4 . By comparing two kinetic models, the pseudo-second-order dynamics model (R = 0.9994) was more suitable than the pseudo-first-order dynamics model (R = 0.9324), obviously describing chemical adsorption process of 2,4-D on MAR@CD-MIP. Table S5 shows the comparison of 2,4-D adsorption by several materials. It could be found that the equilibrium time of UiO-66-NH2 was faster than that of our synthesized MAR@CD-MIP, but the adsorption capacity of MAR@CD-MIP was larger than that of UiO-66-NH2 (Li, et al., 2020). The adsorption capacity and equilibration time of MAR@CD-MIP might be related to the functional groups on the material surface and the CD in the imprinted layer. As described previously, anchoring CD with an amino group (–NH2) on the surface of MAR@CD-MIP provided the active site for adsorption of 2,4-D. The results showed that the MAR@CD-MIP synthesized by us exhibited high adsorption performance on 2,4-D in the field of food detection.

In order to reveal the role of CD in the adsorption process, XPS spectra was measured to explain the effect of CN bond content and N content on the adsorption amount in MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@MIP. It could be observed from Fig. S5 that either MAR@CD-MIP or MAR@MIP was mainly composed of C, N, O and Br. The presence of Br proved the successful preparation of macromolecular initiator. The high-resolution XPS spectra for MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@MIP are shown in Fig. 3 . The peaks at 284.4, 285.2, 286.1 and 288.7 eV in the C1s spectrum were the characteristic adsorption of CC/CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively. Additionally, the proportion of CN in MAR@CD-MIP (Fig. 3 a) was higher than that in MAR@MIP (Fig. 3 d). The peaks at 398.2 and 399.3 eV in the N1s spectrum were the characteristic adsorption of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, respectively. By comparison, the contents of pyrrole N in MAR@CD-MIP (Fig. 3 b) was higher than that in MAR@MIP (Fig. 3 e). Two peaks at 531.5 and 532.8 eV in the O1s spectrum were assigned as characteristic peaks of CO and CO, respectively. In combination with the above FT-IR, elemental analysis and XPS spectra, the introduction of amino groups on the surface of carbon dots would cause the enhancement of 2,4-D adsorption by MAR@CD-MIP. In addition to formation of hydrogen bonds between 4 and VP and 2,4-D, the amino group on the surface of CD could also generate hydrogen bonds with 2,4-D, which might be the key to increase the saturated adsorption capacity of MAR@CD-MIP. All in all, the difference of saturated adsorption capacity between MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@MIP was attributed to the increase of N content and CN of MAR@CD-MIP by the integration of CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence selectivity and sensitivity detection of 2,4-D using MAR@CD-MIP and MAR@CD-NIP
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S6 shows the fluorescence intensity of suspensions with different concentrations of MAR@CD-MIP within 180 min. For suspension with a concentration of 1 mg mL−1, the maximum fluorescence intensity was 37798, but its fluorescence intensity was not stable enough, and the fluorescence intensity decreased by 16 % at 180 min, which made it impossible to conduct subsequent fluorescent quantitative tests. When the concentration of suspension was 0.2 mg mL−1, its fluorescence intensity was 5691. Although its fluorescence intensity was relatively stable, its fluorescence intensity was too low, which is not conducive to the observation of fluorescence quenching. When the concentration of suspension was 0.5 mg mL−1, its fluorescence intensity was 16608, and its fluorescence intensity became relatively stable within 180 min, which is conducive to subsequent quantitative experiments. In conclusion, 0.5 mg mL−1 of MAR@CD-MIP suspension was selected to conduct subsequent fluorescent experiments.

The fluorescence selectivity of MAR@CD-MIP for 2,4-D is shown in Fig. S7 , and it could be observed that MAR@CD-MIP exhibited the most obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon for 2,4-D, with quenching intensity of 40 %. For the other four substances, MAR@CD-MIP did not possess obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon. These results indicate that MAR@CD-MIP exhibited good fluorescence selectivity for 2,4-D. Fluorescence quenching of 2,4-D solution at the concentration of 0–72 μmol/L was carried out at 420 nm. As shown in Fig. 4
a and c, MAR@CD-MIP quenched 2,4-D better than MAR@CD-NIP at the same concentration. 2,4-D was non-specifically adsorbed on the surface of MAR@CD-NIP, and could also cause a slight fluorescence quenching effect on MAR@CD-NIP. The degree of fluorescence quenching could be quantified using the Stern-Volmer equation, as described the following:
(6)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
[ c ]
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity with and without addition of 2,4-D, respectively, K sv is the fluorescence quenching constant, and [c] is the concentration of 2,4-D.

As shown in Fig. 4 b and d, the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and 2,4-D concentration from 18 to 72 μmol/L was acquired. The fitting equation of MAR@CD-MIP was F 0/F = 0.006[c] + 0.928 and the correlation coefficient was 0.9941. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated from LOD = 3σ/s, where σ is the relative standard deviation of fluorescence intensity without the addition of 2,4-D and s is the slope of the calibration curve. Thus, LOD was calculated to be 0.35 μmol/L. As for MAR@CD-NIP, the fitting equation was F 0/F = 0.001[c] + 0.993 and the correlation coefficient was 0.9893. The LOD was calculated to be 4.15 μmol/L. These results demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching efficiency and selectivity of MAR@CD-MIP were significantly enhanced for 2,4-D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of CD fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: In our case, the prepared CD possessed a large number of amino groups. According to previous reports (Jia, Zhang, Li, Shao, Chen, & Yang, 2017; Li, et al., 2020), 2,4-D has an electron-rich aromatic ring structure with strong charge transfer to the electron-deficient amino group, so amino group on the surface of CD could gain electrons and produce Meisenheimer complex with the carboxyl group. In this reaction, the CD energy was transferred to the complex, resulting in fluorescence quenching of CD. Meanwhile, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon could also be explained by molecular orbital theory. The electrons of CD were excited from the ground state to the conduction band by absorbing energy under ultraviolet light, then the excited electrons returns to the ground state from the conduction band. During the return course, CD emits yellow fluorescence. Since 2,4-D could produce hydrogen bonds with the amino groups on the surface of CD, which lead to charge transfer between CD and the carboxyl groups generated the complex, electrons were excited from the ground state to the conduction band and could easily jump to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of the complex. However, the higher energy level of the complex than CD resulted in CD fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the obtained MAR@CD-MIP facilitated the enrichment and detection of 2,4-D from food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of MAR@MIP-CD in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the performance of MAR@CD-MIP, the performance of MAR@CD-MIP was compared by fluorescence spectrophotometry and HPLC. 2,4-D was added to lettuce as spiked samples at the concentrations of 0.64, 1.48 and 6.4 μg kg−1, respectively. The result was shown in Table 1 . It could be found that the recoveries measured by fluorescence spectrophotometry were 97.3–101.7 % with relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.9–5.5 %, which were similar to those by HPLC.

In the detection of HPLC, the linear range was 0–25.6 μg mL−1, and exhibited a good linear relationship (R = 0.999). As shown in Fig. 5
b, 2,4-D was not present in the lettuce samples purchased from local supermarkets. In Fig. 5 c, no peak height was observed. After the treatment with MAR@CD-MIP, 2,4-D in lettuce could be selectively enriched, a higher peak emerged in HPLC (Fig. 5 d). As shown in Table S6
, the recoveries of 2,4-D were 95.5–99.5 %, and the intra-day RSD and inter-day precision were 1.6–5.3 % and 1.1–6.7 %, respectively. These results showed that the prepared MAR@CD-MIP possessed high selectivity for 2,4-D and could be applied in effective enrichment and sensitive detection in real samples. Table S7 lists several materials for selective fluorescence detection of 2,4-D. Compared with other materials, the LOD of MAR@CD-MIP(Wagner, et al., 2018) was slightly higher, but it exhibited wider linear range. It could be seen that the LOD of MIP-2,4-D@SiO2 was 0.005 μmol/L, but the linear range was only 0.02–5 μmol/L. Obviously, the MAR@CD-MIP prepared by us possessed great potential for 2,4-D detection in food.

In summary, a method to prepare fluorescent MIP-based resin with specific adsorption of 2,4-D was proposed by combining CD and surface imprinting technique. The monodisperse resin, MAR@CD-MIP, was synthesized with CD as the fluorescent substance and poly(GMA-co-EDMA) as the substrate and exhibited a highly selective enrichment and sensitive recognition of 2,4-D through the fluorescence quenching phenomenon caused by the electron transfer between 2,4-D and CD. Moreover, the MAR@CD-MIP-based fluorescence detection method was constructed to selectively detect 2,4-D with a wide linear range from 18 to 72 μmol/L. Meanwhile, the adsorption capacity of MAR@CD-MIP was strong with the maximum adsorption capacity of 104 mg g−1. As a result, the dual-functional fluorescent molecularly imprinted resin with adsorption and detection of 2,4-D was successfully developed, provided more possibilities for the application of MAR and CD, but also offered a new option for the detection of 2,4-D in the food products.

Yunjia Xu: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Chao Huang: Formal analysis. Shujuan Ma: Formal analysis. Chunmiao Bo: Conceptualization. Bolin Gong: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Junjie Ou: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support is gratefully acknowledged from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22164001) and the Key research and development program of Ningxia (No. 2022BFE02002) to B. Gong，as well as the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974137), the CAS-Weigao Research & Development Program ([2017]-009) and the National Natural Science Foundation of Ningxia (No. 2021AAC02017) to J. Ou.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.138167.

ce:section.ce:para:
